sections.paragraph:
i.q. לָקַק to lick; German lecken; Arabic لَحِكَ; Syr. ܠܚܟ Peal and Pael id. In Kal it is once used [in speaking of an ox] for to lick away, to eat up or depasture by licking, Num 22:4.

Piel, לִחֵךְ to lick, to eat up by licking, spoken of an ox, Num. loc. cit. (compare Arab. لَسَّ to lick up fodder as an ox), used of fire, 1Ki 18:38 לִחֵךְ עָפָר to lick the dust; hyperb. of one who prostrates himself as a suppliant on the ground, Psa 72:9 Mic 7:17 Isa 49:23.
